extracted_information: Comprehensive strategies for securing a development lifecycle, integrating security into DevOps processes, and utilizing automated testing tools.

SDLC_phases_and_security_integration.goal: Gather and analyze functional and non-functional security requirements, creating tailored guardrails.
practices: Protecting data and integrity as a core requirement.
Covering assurances for user interaction (authentication, authorization, permitted actions, SQL injection prevention).
Covering non-functional requirements (availability, scalability, maintainability) with security choices (segmentation boundaries, firewall ingress/egress).
Documenting security requirements in a specification, reflecting in backlog, explicitly stating investments, tradeoffs, and risks.
Example: Web Application Firewall (WAF) consideration (Azure Front Door, Azure Application Gateway).

SDLC_phases_and_security_integration.goal: Convert security requirements to technical requirements, prevent ambiguity.
practices: Documenting all design decisions in technical specifications.
Defining security dimension of system architecture: security controls on isolation boundaries (segmentation), identity types, encryption methods.

Evaluating platform-provided affordances: understanding shared responsibility with cloud provider, avoiding overlap with native controls (e.g., WAF offloading to Application Gateway/Azure Front Door).
Choosing trusted frameworks, libraries, and supply chain software.
Specifying secure version control.
Sourcing application dependencies from trusted parties; third-party vendors meeting security requirements and sharing disclosure plans.
Maintaining a list of approved/unapproved frameworks, libraries, vendors.
Automating tools to scan dependencies for vulnerabilities.
Determining security design patterns (segmentation, isolation, strong authorization, uniform application security, modern protocols, Quarantine pattern).
Securely storing application secrets: never in source code, use external resources like Azure Key Vault.
Avoiding secrets where possible (e.g., managed identities).
Defining clear test cases for security requirements.
Automating tests in pipelines or including in manual testing.
Initial threat modeling during design, continuous process throughout software evolution.

SDLC_phases_and_security_integration.goal: Prevent security defects and tampering in code, build, and deployment pipelines.
practices: Formal and specialized training in secure coding practices for development team (e.g., XSS, SQL injection).
Requiring training before production source code access.
Internal peer code reviews.
Using security test tools:
Threat modeling.
Static Application Security Testing (SAST) for code analysis, integrated into developer environment.
Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) during runtime, integrated into build pipelines for error checking and attack simulation.
Following industry standards for secure coding practices.
Using linters and code analyzers (e.g., .NET Compiler Platform Roslyn Analyzers) to prevent credential pushing.
Using pipeline add-ons to catch credentials in source code during build process.
Scanning all dependencies (third-party libraries, framework components) as part of Continuous Integration (CI).
Combining security tests with code churn, test results, and coverage.
Writing just enough code: reducing code footprint, reusing validated code/libraries.
Leveraging Azure features (e.g., managed services/PaaS options) to reduce custom code.
Writing code with a deny-all approach by default, allowlists only for entities needing access.
Protecting developer environments: strong network/identity controls for workstations, diligent security updates.
Protecting build agents (highly privileged): authentication, authorization, network segmentation, firewall controls, vulnerability scanning.
Preferring Microsoft-hosted build agents over self-hosted.
Securely storing build machine credentials, regularly removing temporary build artifacts.
Network isolation for build agents (outgoing traffic only, pull model).
Safeguarding source code repository: need-to-know access, security group usage, approval process for access.
Thorough code review process for security vulnerabilities.

SDLC_phases_and_security_integration.goal: Protect build and release environments to prevent malicious code execution.
practices: Maintaining an inventory of all integrated components, stored outside build environment, to check against manifest regularly (preventing backdoor/malware addition).
Pipeline tasks:
Pulling tasks from trusted sources (Azure Marketplace, GitHub tasks/Actions, Microsoft-authored tasks).
Validating tasks running in pipeline security context.
Managing pipeline secrets: proper segmentation for stages, using built-in secret stores, exploring workload/managed identities to avoid secrets.
Keeping different environments separate: production data not in lower environments, no connections from non-prod to prod resources.
Progressive exposure: releasing features to a subset of users, minimizing impact of issues, gradually releasing to broader users.

SDLC_phases_and_security_integration.goal: Last responsible opportunity to fix security gaps.
practices: Keeping a record of the golden image released in production.
Keeping versioned artifacts: catalog of deployed assets and versions for incident triage, mitigation, system recovery.
Comparing versioned assets against published Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) notices using automation.
Emergency fixes: automated pipeline design supporting regular and emergency deployments for rapid security fixes.
Creating emergency processes for accelerated security fixes (communication among teams).
Pipeline allowing quick roll-forward and rollback deployments for security fixes, critical bugs, code updates outside regular lifecycle.

SDLC_phases_and_security_integration.goal: Ensure security posture doesn't decay over time; continuous agile process.
practices: Patch management: keeping software, libraries, infrastructure components up to date.
Continuous improvement: assessing and improving development process via code reviews, feedback, lessons learned, evolving threats.
Decommissioning legacy assets to reduce surface area.
Integrating incident fixes found in production back into the process.
Continuously improving secure coding practices against threat landscape.

definitions.Security_Development_Lifecycle_SDL: A set of practices provided by Microsoft that supports security assurance and compliance requirements.
definitions.Software_Development_Lifecycle_SDLC: A multistage, systematic process for developing software systems.

references.name: Microsoft Security DevOps Azure DevOps extension
Secure development best practices on Azure
Training: Learn how Microsoft supports secure software development as part of a cybersecurity solution

devsecops_strategies.overview: Integrates security into DevOps processes by automating security testing/validation and implementing security pipelines to scan code and Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for vulnerabilities.
core_principles: Hardening code, development environment, and software supply chain.
Ensuring application code is free of security defects for confidentiality, integrity, availability.
Preventing bad implementation or compromised code blocks to strengthen security posture.
Integrating security as a hardening process, context-agnostic to functional requirements.
integration_points_and_automation: Security pipelines to scan code and IaC for vulnerabilities.
Integrating SAST into developer environments for real-time vulnerability detection.
Integrating DAST into build pipelines for runtime checks and attack simulation.
Automating dependency scanning as part of CI.
Automating credential detection via linters, code analyzers, and pipeline add-ons.
Using tools like Defender for DevOps and SAST tools (GitHub Advanced Security, Azure DevOps) to track security scores.
Automating comparisons of versioned assets against CVE notices.

types_of_testing: Static Application Security Testing (SAST): analyzes code for vulnerabilities, integrated into developer environment for real-time detection.
Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST): during runtime, checks for errors in security domains, simulates attacks, integrated into build pipelines.
Dependency scanning: scanning third-party libraries and framework components as part of CI.
Credential detection: linters, code analyzers (.NET Compiler Platform Roslyn Analyzers), pipeline add-ons.
Combination of tests for comprehensive security testing.
integration_points: Build pipelines (DAST, dependency scanning, credential detection).
tools_mentioned: GitHub Advanced Security (for finding credentials, SAST)
OWASP source code analysis tools (for finding credentials)
Mend Bolt (for open-source code security validation)
OWASP Dependency-Check (for open-source code security validation)
GitHub Dependabot (for open-source code security validation)
Microsoft Security DevOps Azure DevOps extension (for open-source code security validation)
